title: CLAUDE.md
text: IOBR (Immune Oncology Biological Research) R package tumor microenvironment (TME) analysis. provides: TME Deconvolution: 11 methods (CIBERSORT, TIMER, xCell, MCPcounter, ESTIMATE, EPIC, IPS, quanTIseq, SVR, lsei) Signature Scoring: PCA, z-score, ssGSEA methods 322+ curated gene signatures Visualization: Survival plots, correlation heatmaps, box plots, forest plots Data Preprocessing: Batch correction, normalization, format conversion

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Expression sets (eset): Matrix/data.frame genes rownames, samples columns. Expects HGNC gene symbols (Ensembl IDs). Signature collection: Lists gene vectors (e.g., signature_collection 323 signatures) Deconvolution results: Data frames ID column + cell fraction columns (suffixed method name)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Stored R/sysdata.rda (internal) data/ (exported): lm22: CIBERSORT reference matrix (22 immune cell types) signature_collection: 323 curated gene signatures citations xCell.data, mcp_genes, quantiseq_data: Method-specific reference data TRef/BRef: EPIC reference matrices

title: Multidimensional Decoding Tumor Microenvironment for Immuno-Oncology Research
text: IOBR acronym Immuno-Oncology Biological Research perform multi-omics immuno-oncology biological research decipher tumour microenvironment signatures clinical translation.

title: Multidimensional Decoding Tumor Microenvironment for Immuno-Oncology Research
text: IOBR encompasses six functional modules: Transcriptome data prepare module : pre-procession transcriptome data, well pertinent batch statistical analyses; TME deconvolution signature estimation module : estimation signature scores identification phenotype relevant signatures, along decoding immune contexture; TME interaction module : clustering TME characteristics analyzing receptor-ligand interactions; Genome TME interaction module : analysis signature associated mutations; TME data visualization Statistical analysis module : visual representation statistical examination TME data; TME modeling module : fast model construction assessment model performance.

title: Multidimensional Decoding Tumor Microenvironment for Immuno-Oncology Research
text: IOBR integrates eight open-source deconvolution methodologies: CIBERSORT , ESTIMATE , quanTIseq , TIMER , IPS , MCPCounter , xCell EPIC . addition, 323 published signature gene sets collected IOBR covering tumour microenvironment , tumour metabolism , m6A , exosomes , microsatellite instability tertiary lymphoid structure. IOBR used three computational methods calculate signature score, including PCA , z-score ssGSEA.

title: Multidimensional Decoding Tumor Microenvironment for Immuno-Oncology Research
text: IOBR integrates visualization function, including boxplots , heatmaps , percentage bar charts , scatter plots , KM plot , PCA plot etc.

title: Multidimensional Decoding Tumor Microenvironment for Immuno-Oncology Research

title: IOBR Package Development Instructions
text: Use modern R installation manager rig package installer pak optimal development experience: Timing: rig installation ~1 minute, R installation ~5-8 minutes
code: # Install rig (R Installation Manager) curl -Ls https://github.com/r-lib/rig/releases/download/latest/rig-linux-latest.tar.gz | sudo tar xz -C /usr/local  # Install latest R release sudo rig add release  # Verify installation rig list R --version

title: IOBR Package Development Instructions
text: Timing: Dependencies installation ~4-6 minutes, dev tools ~2-3 minutes
code: # Navigate to package directory cd /path/to/IOBR  # Install all package dependencies using pak (much faster than install.packages) R --slave -e "pak::local_install_deps()"  # Install additional development tools R --slave -e "pak::pak(c('devtools', 'knitr', 'rmarkdown', 'testthat', 'pkgdown', 'roxygen2', 'styler', 'lintr'))"

title: IOBR Package Development Instructions

what: Immune Oncology Biological Research in R
title: Immune Oncology Biological Research in R
text: IOBR comprehensive R package designed immuno-oncology research, providing one-stop solution tumor microenvironment (TME) deconvolution, signature analysis, integrated visualization. integrates multiple state---art algorithms curated gene sets facilitate -depth analysis tumor immunity.

title: Immune Oncology Biological Research in R
text: IOBR Workflow IOBR Package

title: Immune Oncology Biological Research in R
text: 322+ published signature gene sets covering TME, metabolism, m6A, exosomes, microsatellite instability, tertiary lymphoid structure, Easy access signature genes source citations Flexible signature management customization

title: Immune Oncology Biological Research in R
text: Integrates 8 cutting-edge TME decoding methodologies: CIBERSORT - Cell-type identification estimating relative subsets RNA transcripts TIMER - Tumor Immune Estimation Resource xCell - Digital portrayal tissue cellular heterogeneity MCPcounter - Estimation immune stromal cell populations ESTIMATE - Inference tumor purity stromal/immune cell admixture EPIC - Enumeration cancer immune cell types IPS - Immunophenoscore calculation quanTIseq - Quantification tumor-infiltrating immune cells

title: Immune Oncology Biological Research in R
text: Three robust computational methods signature scoring: PCA - Principal Component Analysis z-score - Standardized expression scoring ssGSEA - Single-sample Gene Set Enrichment Analysis

title: Immune Oncology Biological Research in R
text: Data preprocessing normalization Batch effect correction Feature selection dimensionality reduction Survival analysis risk modeling Statistical testing visualization

title: Immune Oncology Biological Research in R
text: Batch survival analysis plots Subgroup characteristic visualization Correlation heatmaps scatter plots Forest plots biomarker validation Customizable themes color palettes

However, improvements may introduce code instability inconsistency. need reproduce previous analysis results, please install use previously tagged versions https://github.com/IOBR/IOBR/tags.

text: Using Conda Mamba provides clean, isolated environment system dependencies pre-installed: install IOBR R:
code: # Create a new conda environment with R and essential dependencies mamba create -n r45 -c conda-forge -c bioconda \   r-base=4.5 r-xml r-magick r-cairo r-gdtools r-remotes r-tidyverse r-biocmanager  # Activate the environment mamba activate r45 BiocManager::install("IOBR/IOBR")

title: Immune Oncology Biological Research in R
text: IOBR downloads large datasets GitHub releases caches locally. can customize cache location: permanently set custom cache directory, add ~/.Rprofile: R fails download, can manually download data GitHub GitHub acceleration sites, configure cache directory.
code: # Check current cache directory get_iobr_cache_dir()  # Set a custom cache directory (persists for current R session) set_iobr_cache_dir("~/my_iobr_cache")  # Or set via options (useful in .Rprofile for permanent setting) options(IOBR.cache_dir = "~/my_iobr_cache")  # Clear cached data clear_iobr_cache()  # Reset to default cache location reset_iobr_cache_dir() options(IOBR.cache_dir = "/path/to/your/cache")

what: Calculate AUC for Binomial Model — BinomialAUC
title: Calculate AUC for Binomial Model — BinomialAUC
text: Computes Area ROC Curve (AUC) model predictions using ROCR package. Handles binary classification models glmnet.

title: Calculate AUC for Binomial Model — BinomialAUC
code: BinomialAUC(model, newx, s, acture.y)

title: Calculate AUC for Binomial Model — BinomialAUC
text: model Fitted glmnet model object. newx New data matrix prediction. s Lambda value prediction (e.g., "lambda.min" numeric). acture.y Actual binary outcomes (numeric 0/1 factor).

title: Calculate AUC for Binomial Model — BinomialAUC
text: Numeric AUC value 0 1.

title: Calculate AUC for Binomial Model — BinomialAUC
code: if (requireNamespace("glmnet", quietly = TRUE) &&   requireNamespace("ROCR", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(123)   train_data <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 5), ncol = 5)   train_outcome <- rbinom(100, 1, 0.5)   test_data <- matrix(rnorm(50 * 5), ncol = 5)   test_outcome <- rbinom(50, 1, 0.5)   fitted_model <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(train_data, train_outcome, family = "binomial", nfolds = 5)   auc_value <- BinomialAUC(fitted_model, test_data, fitted_model$lambda.min, test_outcome)   print(auc_value) } #> [1] 0.4879227

title: Binomial Model Construction — BinomialModel
text: Constructs evaluates binomial logistic regression models using Lasso Ridge regularization. Processes input data, scales features specified, splits data training/testing sets, fits Lasso Ridge models. Optionally generates AUC plots model evaluation.

title: Binomial Model Construction — BinomialModel
code: BinomialModel(   x,   y,   seed = 123456,   scale = TRUE,   train_ratio = 0.7,   nfold = 10,   plot = TRUE,   palette = "jama",   cols = NULL )

title: Binomial Model Construction — BinomialModel
text: x data frame containing sample ID features. First column must sample ID. y data frame first column sample ID second column outcome (numeric factor). seed Integer random seed. Default `123456`. scale Logical indicating whether scale features. Default `TRUE`. train_ratio Numeric 0 1 training proportion. Default `0.7`. nfold Integer cross-validation folds. Default `10`. plot Logical indicating whether generate AUC plots. Default `TRUE`. palette Character string color palette. Default `"jama"`. cols Optional color vector ROC curves. Default `NULL`.

title: Binomial Model Construction — BinomialModel
text: List containing: lasso_result Lasso model results ridge_result Ridge model results train.x Training data IDs

title: Binomial Model Construction — BinomialModel
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Binomial Model Construction — BinomialModel
code: set.seed(123) x <- data.frame(   ID = paste0("Sample", 1:50),   Feature1 = rnorm(50),   Feature2 = rnorm(50),   Feature3 = rnorm(50) ) y <- data.frame(   ID = x$ID,   Outcome = factor(rbinom(50, 1, 0.5)) ) result <- BinomialModel(x = x, y = y, plot = FALSE, nfold = 5) #> ℹ Processing data #> ℹ Splitting data into training and test sets #> ℹ Running LASSO #> ℹ Running RIDGE REGRESSION #> ✔ Model fitting complete str(result, max.level = 1) #> List of 4 #>  $ lasso_result:List of 3 #>  $ ridge_result:List of 3 #>  $ train.x     :'data.frame':	35 obs. of  4 variables: #>  $ plots       : NULL

title: CIBERSORT Deconvolution Algorithm — CIBERSORT
text: analytical tool estimate cell type abundances mixed cell populations using gene expression data.

title: CIBERSORT Deconvolution Algorithm — CIBERSORT
code: CIBERSORT(   sig_matrix = NULL,   mixture_file,   perm,   QN = TRUE,   absolute = FALSE,   abs_method = "sig.score",   parallel = FALSE,   num_cores = 2,   seed = NULL )

title: CIBERSORT Deconvolution Algorithm — CIBERSORT
text: sig_matrix Cell type GEP barcode matrix: row 1 = sample labels; column 1 = gene symbols; missing values; default = LM22.txt download CIBERSORT (https://cibersortx.stanford.edu/runcibersort.php) mixture_file GEP matrix: row 1 = sample labels; column 1 = gene symbols; missing values perm Set permutations statistical analysis (>=100 permutations recommended). QN Quantile normalization input mixture (default = TRUE) absolute Run CIBERSORT absolute mode (default = FALSE) - note cell subsets scaled absolute levels   represented fractions (derive default output,   normalize absolute levels sum 1 mixture sample) - sum cell subsets mixture sample added   output ('Absolute score'). LM22 used, score capture   total immune content. abs_method absolute set TRUE, choose method: '.sumto1' 'sig.score' - sig.score = mixture sample, define S median expression   level genes signature matrix divided median   expression level genes mixture. Multiple cell subset   fractions S. - .sumto1 = remove sum 1 constraint parallel Logical. Enable parallel execution? (default = FALSE) num_cores Integer. Number cores use parallel = TRUE (default = 2) seed Integer. Random seed reproducible permutation testing. NULL (default), uses current random state. Set specific value (e.g., 123) reproducible results across runs. Applies parallel serial permutation.

title: CIBERSORT Deconvolution Algorithm — CIBERSORT
text: matrix object containing estimated cibersort-cell fractions,   p-values, correlation coefficients, RMSE values.

title: CIBERSORT Deconvolution Algorithm — CIBERSORT
text: Aaron M. Newman, Stanford University (amnewman@stanford.edu)

title: CIBERSORT Deconvolution Algorithm — CIBERSORT
code: # Simulate data set.seed(123) sim_sig <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 5), 100, 5) rownames(sim_sig) <- paste0("Gene", 1:100) colnames(sim_sig) <- paste0("Cell", 1:5)  sim_mixture <- as.data.frame(matrix(   rnorm(100 * 3), 100, 3 )) rownames(sim_mixture) <- paste0("Gene", 1:100) colnames(sim_mixture) <- paste0("Sample", 1:3)  # Run deconvolution result <- CIBERSORT(   sig_matrix = sim_sig,   mixture_file = sim_mixture,   perm = 10, QN = FALSE, absolute = FALSE,   parallel = FALSE ) if (!is.null(result)) head(result) #>             Cell1     Cell2      Cell3     Cell4     Cell5 P-value Correlation #> Sample1 0.2661124 0.3560011 0.09902726 0.0000000 0.2788592     0.2  0.14713328 #> Sample2 0.1987843 0.3010143 0.50020143 0.0000000 0.0000000     0.8  0.04060941 #> Sample3 0.4232823 0.1873284 0.00000000 0.3893893 0.0000000     0.1  0.17900877 #>             RMSE #> Sample1 1.031594 #> Sample2 1.132430 #> Sample3 1.062058

title: Calculate Performance Metrics — CalculatePref

code: CalculatePref(model, newx, s, acture.y)

title: Calculate Performance Metrics — CalculatePref
text: model Fitted glmnet model. newx New data matrix prediction. s Lambda value prediction. acture.y Actual binary outcomes.

title: Calculate Performance Metrics — CalculatePref
text: ROCR performance object containing TPR FPR values.

title: Calculate Performance Metrics — CalculatePref
code: if (requireNamespace("glmnet", quietly = TRUE) &&   requireNamespace("ROCR", quietly = TRUE)) {   fitted_model <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(matrix(rnorm(100), ncol = 2), rbinom(50, 1, 0.5), nfolds = 3)   perf <- CalculatePref(fitted_model, matrix(rnorm(20), ncol = 2), "lambda.min", rbinom(10, 1, 0.5)) }

title: Calculate Time-Dependent ROC Curve — CalculateTimeROC
text: Computes time-dependent ROC curve survival models using `timeROC` package. Evaluates predictive accuracy specified time quantile.

title: Calculate Time-Dependent ROC Curve — CalculateTimeROC
code: CalculateTimeROC(model, newx, s, acture.y, modelname, time_prob = 0.9)

title: Calculate Time-Dependent ROC Curve — CalculateTimeROC
text: model fitted survival model object. newx matrix data frame new data prediction. s Lambda value prediction. acture.y Data frame `time` `status` columns. modelname Character string model identification. time_prob Numeric quantile ROC calculation. Default `0.9`.

title: Calculate Time-Dependent ROC Curve — CalculateTimeROC
text: object class `timeROC` containing ROC curve information.

title: Calculate Time-Dependent ROC Curve — CalculateTimeROC
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Calculate Time-Dependent ROC Curve — CalculateTimeROC
code: if (requireNamespace("glmnet", quietly = TRUE) &&   requireNamespace("survival", quietly = TRUE) &&   requireNamespace("timeROC", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(survival)   dat <- na.omit(lung[, c("time", "status", "age", "sex", "ph.ecog")])   dat$status <- dat$status - 1   x <- as.matrix(dat[, c("age", "sex", "ph.ecog")])   y <- Surv(dat$time, dat$status)   fit <- glmnet::glmnet(x, y, family = "cox")   actual_outcome <- data.frame(time = dat$time, status = dat$status)   roc_info <- CalculateTimeROC(     model = fit, newx = x, s = 0.01, acture.y = actual_outcome,     modelname = "glmnet Cox Model", time_prob = 0.5   )   print(roc_info$AUC) } #> Warning: Starting in glmnet 5.1, the default Cox tie-handling method will change from 'breslow' to 'efron' (matching survival::coxph). To silence this message and lock in the v5.0 default, pass cox.ties = 'breslow' explicitly. To preview the v5.1 behavior, pass cox.ties = 'efron'. #>       t=0     t=259  #>        NA 0.6791684

what: Core Algorithm for CIBERSORT Deconvolution — CoreAlg
title: Core Algorithm for CIBERSORT Deconvolution — CoreAlg
text: Performs nu-regression using support vector machines (SVM) estimate cell type proportions. core computational engine CIBERSORT, using nu-SVR linear kernel decompose mixed gene expression signals.

title: Core Algorithm for CIBERSORT Deconvolution — CoreAlg
code: CoreAlg(X, y, absolute, abs_method)

title: Core Algorithm for CIBERSORT Deconvolution — CoreAlg
text: X Matrix data frame containing signature matrix (predictor variables). Rows genes, columns cell types. y Numeric vector containing mixture sample expression (response variable). absolute Logical indicating whether use absolute space weights. Default FALSE (relative proportions). abs_method String specifying method absolute space weights: `"sig.score"` `".sumto1"`.

title: Core Algorithm for CIBERSORT Deconvolution — CoreAlg
text: List containing: w Estimated cell type weights/proportions mix_rmse Root mean squared error deconvolution mix_r Correlation coefficient observed predicted mixture

title: Core Algorithm for CIBERSORT Deconvolution — CoreAlg
code: # Simulate data set.seed(123) X <- matrix(rnorm(100), nrow = 10) rownames(X) <- paste0("Gene", 1:10) colnames(X) <- paste0("Cell", 1:10) y <- rnorm(10) names(y) <- paste0("Gene", 1:10)  # Run core algorithm result <- CoreAlg(X, y, absolute = FALSE, abs_method = "sig.score") if (!is.null(result)) str(result) #> List of 3 #>  $ w       : num [1, 1:10] 0.373 0.2 0.301 0 0 ... #>   ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>   .. ..$ : NULL #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:10] "Cell1" "Cell2" "Cell3" "Cell4" ... #>  $ mix_rmse: num 0.651 #>  $ mix_r   : num 0.582

what: Estimate the proportion of immune and cancer cells. — EPIC
title: Estimate the proportion of immune and cancer cells. — EPIC
text: EPIC takes input bulk gene expression data (RNA-seq) returns  proportion mRNA cells composing various samples.

title: Estimate the proportion of immune and cancer cells. — EPIC
code: EPIC(   bulk,   reference = NULL,   mRNA_cell = NULL,   mRNA_cell_sub = NULL,   sigGenes = NULL,   scaleExprs = TRUE,   withOtherCells = TRUE,   constrainedSum = TRUE,   rangeBasedOptim = FALSE )

title: Estimate the proportion of immune and cancer cells. — EPIC
text: bulk matrix (nGenes x nSamples) genes expression bulk sample (counts given TPM, RPKM FPKM using prebuilt reference profiles). matrix needs rownames telling gene names (corresponds gene symbol prebuilt reference profiles (e.g. CD8A, MS4A1) - conversion IDs performed moment). advised keep genes bulk instead subset signature genes (except scaleExprs = FALSE case make difference). reference (optional): string list defining reference cells. can take multiple formats: - `NULL`: use default reference profiles genes signature TRef - character: either "BRef" "TRef" use reference cells genes signature corresponding datasets (see BRef TRef) - list containing:   - `$refProfiles`: matrix (nGenes x nCellTypes)   reference cells genes expression (include column '  cells' (representing usually cancer cells profile   usually conserved samples); rownames needs   defined well colnames giving names gene   reference cell types respectively. advised keep genes   refProfiles matrix instead subset signature genes   - `$sigGenes`: character vector gene names use   signature - sigGenes can also given direct input EPIC   function   - `$refProfiles.var` (optional): matrix (nGenes x   nCellTypes) variability gene expression   cell type, used define weights gene   optimization (absent, assume identical variability   genes cells) - needs dimnames   refProfiles mRNA_cell (optional): named numeric vector: tells (arbitrary units) amount mRNA reference cells uncharacterized (cancer) cell. Two names special meaning: "otherCells" - used mRNA/cell value "cells" sample (.e. cell type reference gene expression profile) ; default - used mRNA/cell cells reference profiles specific value given mRNA_cell (.e. mRNA_cell=c(Bcells=2, NKcells=2.1, otherCells=3.5, default=1), refProfiles described Bcells, NKcells Tcells, use value 3.5 "otherCells" reference profile default value 1 Tcells computing cell fractions). note: data tpm, mRNA per cell ideally correspond number transcripts per cell. mRNA_cell_sub (optional): can given instead mRNA_cell (addition ). also named numeric vector, used replace mRNA/cell values cell types (add values new cell types). values given mRNA_cell_sub overwrite default values well might given mRNA_cell. sigGenes (optional): character vector gene names use signature deconvolution. principle given reference "reference$sigGenes" give value input variable, signature genes used instead ones given reference profile. scaleExprs (optional, default TRUE): boolean telling bulk samples reference gene expression profiles rescaled based list genes common (rescaling recommanded). withOtherCells (optional, default TRUE): EPIC allow additional cell type gene expression reference profile available bulk assumed composed cells reference profiles. constrainedSum (optional, default TRUE): tells sum cell types constrained < 1. withOtherCells=FALSE, additionally constrain sum cell types reference profiles must > 0.99. rangeBasedOptim (optional): FALSE (default), least square optimization performed described   Racle et al., 2017, eLife, recommanded. variable TRUE, EPIC uses variability gene reference profiles another way: instead defining weights (based variability) fit gene, define range values accessible gene (based gene expression value reference profile +/- variability values). error optimization tries minimize much predicted gene expression outside allowed range values.

title: Estimate the proportion of immune and cancer cells. — EPIC
text: list 3 matrices: - `mRNAProportions`: (nSamples x (nCellTypes+1)) proportion mRNA coming cell types ref profile + uncharacterized cell - `cellFractions`: (nSamples x (nCellTypes+1)) gives proportion cells cell type accounting mRNA / cell value - `fit.gof`: (nSamples x 12) matrix telling quality fit signature genes sample. tells minimization converged, info fit comparing measured gene expression sigGenes vs predicted gene expression sigGenes

title: Estimate the proportion of immune and cancer cells. — EPIC
text: function uses constrained least square minimization estimate proportion cell type reference profile another uncharacterized cell type bulk gene expression samples. names genes bulk samples, reference samples gene signature list need format (gene symbols used predefined reference profiles). full list gene names need exactly reference bulk samples: EPIC use intersection genes. case duplicate gene names, EPIC use median value per duplicate - want consider cases differently, can remove duplicates calling EPIC.

title: Estimate the proportion of immune and cancer cells. — EPIC
code: melanoma_counts <- matrix(abs(rnorm(80)), nrow = 20, ncol = 4) rownames(melanoma_counts) <- paste0("Gene", 1:20) colnames(melanoma_counts) <- paste0("Sample", 1:4) mock_ref <- list(   refProfiles = matrix(abs(rnorm(80)), nrow = 20, ncol = 4),   sigGenes = paste0("Gene", 1:10) ) rownames(mock_ref$refProfiles) <- paste0("Gene", 1:20) colnames(mock_ref$refProfiles) <- c("Bcells", "CD4T", "CD8T", "NK") res1 <- EPIC(melanoma_counts, reference = mock_ref) #> Warning: 'refProfiles.var' not defined; using identical weights for all genes #> Warning: there are few genes in common between the bulk samples and reference cells:20, so the data scaling might be an issue #> ℹ Trying mirror 1/12: <https://github.com> #> ✔ Download complete: "mRNA_cell_default" #> Warning: mRNA_cell value unknown for some cell types: CD4T, CD8T, NK - using the default value of 0.4 for these but this might bias the true cell proportions from all cell types. if (!is.null(res1)) head(res1$cellFractions) #>            Bcells         CD4T         CD8T           NK   otherCells #> Sample1 0.4230775 3.006759e-02 4.844949e-01 1.684242e-08 6.236003e-02 #> Sample2 0.2068297 2.951330e-04 1.739604e-01 6.189147e-01 2.347104e-09 #> Sample3 0.4371192 4.646099e-09 5.628792e-01 8.043256e-08 1.460648e-06 #> Sample4 0.3613680 6.895614e-09 4.523433e-08 5.324749e-01 1.061570e-01

title: Elastic Net Model Fitting — Enet
text: Fits elastic net model cross-validation find optimal alpha lambda. Searches across grid alpha values (0 1) lambda values minimize cross-validation error.

title: Elastic Net Model Fitting — Enet
code: Enet(train.x, train.y, lambdamax, nfold = 10)

title: Elastic Net Model Fitting — Enet
text: train.x Training predictors matrix. train.y Training binary outcomes (0/1 factor). lambdamax Maximum lambda value grid search. nfold Number CV folds. Default `10`.

title: Elastic Net Model Fitting — Enet
text: List containing: chose_alpha Optimal alpha value (0-1) chose_lambda Optimal lambda value

title: Elastic Net Model Fitting — Enet
code: if (requireNamespace("glmnet", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(123)   train_data <- matrix(rnorm(50 * 5), ncol = 5)   train_outcome <- rbinom(50, 1, 0.5)   result <- Enet(train.x = train_data, train.y = train_outcome, lambdamax = 1, nfold = 5) }

title: Get Outlier Genes — GetOutlierGenes
code: tf1 <- tempfile(fileext = ".tsv") tf2 <- tempfile(fileext = ".tsv")  expr1 <- data.frame(   gene = c("GeneA", "GeneB", "GeneC", "GeneD", "GeneE", "GeneF"),   Sample1 = c(10, 50, 30, 80, 60, 20),   Sample2 = c(15, 40, 25, 90, 55, 10) )  expr2 <- data.frame(   gene = c("GeneA", "GeneB", "GeneC", "GeneD", "GeneE", "GeneF"),   Sample3 = c(100, 20, 10, 60, 30, 80),   Sample4 = c(95, 25, 15, 70, 35, 85) )  write.table(expr1, tf1, sep = "\t", row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE) write.table(expr2, tf2, sep = "\t", row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE)  cancers <- data.frame(ExpressionFiles = c(tf1, tf2)) outlier_genes <- GetOutlierGenes(cancers)

title: Calculate Immunophenoscore (IPS) — IPS_calculation
text: Calculates Immunophenoscore (IPS) gene expression data. IPS composite score measuring immunophenotype based four major categories: MHC molecules, immunomodulators, effector cells, suppressor cells.

title: Calculate Immunophenoscore (IPS) — IPS_calculation
code: IPS_calculation(eset, project = NULL, plot = FALSE)

title: Calculate Immunophenoscore (IPS) — IPS_calculation
text: eset Gene expression matrix official human gene symbols (HGNC) rownames. Expression values log2(TPM+1) transformed max value > 100. project Character string project identifier. Default NULL. plot Logical. Whether generate immunophenogram plots. Default FALSE.

title: Calculate Immunophenoscore (IPS) — IPS_calculation
text: Data frame containing: MHC MHC molecules score EC Effector cells score SC Suppressor cells score CP Checkpoints/Immunomodulators score AZ Aggregate score (sum MHC, CP, EC, SC) IPS Immunophenoscore (0-10 scale)

title: Calculate Immunophenoscore (IPS) — IPS_calculation
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ example_genes <- c(   "HLA-A", "HLA-B", "HLA-C", "CD274", "PDCD1", "CTLA4",   "CD8A", "CD8B", "GZMB", "PRF1", "FOXP3", "IL10" ) sim_eset <- as.data.frame(matrix(   rnorm(length(example_genes) * 5, mean = 5, sd = 2),   nrow = length(example_genes), ncol = 5 )) rownames(sim_eset) <- example_genes colnames(sim_eset) <- paste0("Sample", 1:5) ips_result <- IPS_calculation(eset = sim_eset, project = "Example", plot = FALSE) if (!is.null(ips_result)) head(ips_result) } # }

Default `"ensembl"`. cancer_type Character string specifying cancer type easier. Default `"pancan"` pan-cancer analysis.

title: Calculate Ligand-Receptor Interaction Scores — LR_cal
text: Data frame containing ligand-receptor interaction scores   sample IDs row names.

title: Calculate Ligand-Receptor Interaction Scores — LR_cal
text: Lapuente-Santana, van Genderen, M., Hilbers, P., Finotello, F., & Eduati, F. (2021). Interpretable systems biomarkers predict response immune-checkpoint inhibitors. Patterns (New York, N.Y.), 2(8), 100293. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.patter.2021.100293

title: Calculate Ligand-Receptor Interaction Scores — LR_cal
code: # LR_cal requires HGNC gene symbols as rownames # Create a simple example with gene symbols example_genes <- c(   "TGFB1", "EGFR", "VEGFA", "PDGFB", "FGF2", "CXCL12",   "CXCR4", "IL6", "IL6R", "TNF", "TNFRSF1A", "IFNG" ) sim_eset <- as.data.frame(matrix(   rnorm(length(example_genes) * 10, mean = 5, sd = 2),   nrow = length(example_genes), ncol = 10 )) rownames(sim_eset) <- example_genes colnames(sim_eset) <- paste0("Sample", 1:10) if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ if (requireNamespace("easier", quietly = TRUE)) {   tryCatch({     lr <- LR_cal(eset = sim_eset, data_type = "tpm")     head(lr)   }, error = function(e) {     message("Example skipped: could not download ExperimentHub data")   }) } } # }

text: Estimates abundance different immune stromal cell populations using MCP-counter method. Works various gene identifiers including Affymetrix probesets, HUGO gene symbols, Entrez IDs, Ensembl IDs.

title: MCP-counter Cell Population Abundance Estimation — MCPcounter.estimate
code: MCPcounter.estimate(   expression,   featuresType = c("affy133P2_probesets", "HUGO_symbols", "ENTREZ_ID", "ENSEMBL_ID"),   probesets = read.table(system.file("extdata/probesets.txt", package = "IOBR"), sep =     "\t", stringsAsFactors = FALSE, colClasses = "character"),   genes = read.table(system.file("extdata/genes.txt", package = "IOBR"), sep = "\t",     stringsAsFactors = FALSE, header = TRUE, colClasses = "character", check.names =     FALSE) )

title: MCP-counter Cell Population Abundance Estimation — MCPcounter.estimate
text: expression Matrix data.frame features rows samples columns. featuresType Type identifiers expression features. Options: "affy133P2_probesets", "HUGO_symbols", "ENTREZ_ID", "ENSEMBL_ID". Default "affy133P2_probesets". probesets Probesets data table. Default loads GitHub. genes Genes data table. Default loads GitHub.

title: MCP-counter Cell Population Abundance Estimation — MCPcounter.estimate
text: Matrix cell populations rows samples columns.

text: Etienne Becht

title: Plot AUC ROC Curves — PlotAUC
text: Generates ROC curves model evaluation comparing training testing performance lambda.min lambda.1se. Creates ggplot visualization AUC values legend.

title: Plot AUC ROC Curves — PlotAUC
code: PlotAUC(   train.x,   train.y,   test.x,   test.y,   model,   modelname,   cols = NULL,   palette = "jama" )

title: Plot AUC ROC Curves — PlotAUC
text: train.x Training predictors matrix. train.y Training outcomes (binary factor). test.x Testing predictors matrix. test.y Testing outcomes (binary factor). model Fitted cv.glmnet model. modelname Character string plot title. cols Optional color vector ROC curves. palette Color palette name IOBR palettes. Default `"jama"`.

title: Plot AUC ROC Curves — PlotAUC
text: ggplot object ROC curves.

title: Plot AUC ROC Curves — PlotAUC
code: if (requireNamespace("glmnet", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(123)   train_data <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 5), ncol = 5)   train_outcome <- rbinom(100, 1, 0.5)   test_data <- matrix(rnorm(50 * 5), ncol = 5)   test_outcome <- rbinom(50, 1, 0.5)   fitted_model <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(train_data, train_outcome, family = "binomial", nfolds = 5)   p <- PlotAUC(train_data, train_outcome, test_data, test_outcome, fitted_model, "MyModel")   print(p) }

title: Plot Time-Dependent ROC Curves — PlotTimeROC

title: Plot Time-Dependent ROC Curves — PlotTimeROC
text: train.x Matrix data frame training predictors. train.y Training survival outcomes (time status). test.x Matrix data frame testing predictors. test.y Testing survival outcomes (time status). model Fitted survival model object. modelname Character string model identification. cols Optional vector colors plotting. palette Character string specifying color palette. Default `"jama"`.

title: Plot Time-Dependent ROC Curves — PlotTimeROC
text: `ggplot` object representing ROC curve plot.

title: Plot Time-Dependent ROC Curves — PlotTimeROC
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Plot Time-Dependent ROC Curves — PlotTimeROC
code: if (requireNamespace("glmnet", quietly = TRUE) &&   requireNamespace("survival", quietly = TRUE) &&   requireNamespace("timeROC", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(survival)   set.seed(123)   train_x <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 5), ncol = 5)   train_y <- data.frame(time = rexp(100), status = rbinom(100, 1, 0.5))   test_x <- matrix(rnorm(50 * 5), ncol = 5)   test_y <- data.frame(time = rexp(50), status = rbinom(50, 1, 0.5))   fit <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(train_x, Surv(train_y$time, train_y$status), family = "cox")   p <- PlotTimeROC(train_x, train_y, test_x, test_y, fit, "Cox Model")   print(p) } #> Warning: Starting in glmnet 5.1, the default Cox tie-handling method will change from 'breslow' to 'efron' (matching survival::coxph). what: Calculate Time-Dependent AUC for Survival Models — PrognosticAUC
title: Calculate Time-Dependent AUC for Survival Models — PrognosticAUC
text: Evaluates prognostic ability survival model calculating time-dependent AUC 30th 90th percentiles survival time. thresholds assess short-term long-term predictive accuracy.

title: Calculate Time-Dependent AUC for Survival Models — PrognosticAUC
code: PrognosticAUC(model, newx, s, acture.y)

title: Calculate Time-Dependent AUC for Survival Models — PrognosticAUC
text: model fitted survival model object capable generating risk scores. newx matrix data frame new data prediction. s Lambda value prediction. Can numeric `"lambda.min"`/`"lambda.1se"`. acture.y Data frame `time` `status` columns.

title: Calculate Time-Dependent AUC for Survival Models — PrognosticAUC
text: data frame AUC values 30th (`probs.3`) 90th (`probs.9`) percentiles.

title: Calculate Time-Dependent AUC for Survival Models — PrognosticAUC
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Calculate Time-Dependent AUC for Survival Models — PrognosticAUC
code: if (requireNamespace("glmnet", quietly = TRUE) &&   requireNamespace("survival", quietly = TRUE) &&   requireNamespace("timeROC", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(survival)   set.seed(123)   x <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 5), ncol = 5)   y <- Surv(rexp(100), rbinom(100, 1, 0.5))   fit <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(x, y, family = "cox")   acture_y <- data.frame(time = y[, 1], status = y[, 2])   auc_results <- PrognosticAUC(fit, newx = x, s = "lambda.min", acture.y = acture_y) } #> Warning: Starting in glmnet 5.1, the default Cox tie-handling method will change from 'breslow' to 'efron' (matching survival::coxph). what: Build Prognostic Models Using LASSO and Ridge Regression — PrognosticModel
title: Build Prognostic Models Using LASSO and Ridge Regression — PrognosticModel
text: Prepares data, splits training testing sets, fits LASSO Ridge regression models survival analysis. Evaluates model performance using cross-validation optionally generates time-dependent ROC curves visual assessment predictive accuracy.

title: Build Prognostic Models Using LASSO and Ridge Regression — PrognosticModel
code: PrognosticModel(   x,   y,   scale = FALSE,   seed = 123456,   train_ratio = 0.7,   nfold = 10,   plot = TRUE,   palette = "jama",   cols = NULL )

title: Build Prognostic Models Using LASSO and Ridge Regression — PrognosticModel
text: x matrix data frame predictor variables (features). y data frame survival outcomes two columns: survival time event status. scale Logical indicating whether scale predictor variables. Default `FALSE`. seed Integer seed random number generation ensure reproducibility. Default `123456`. train_ratio Numeric proportion data training (e.g., 0.7). Default `0.7`. nfold Integer number folds cross-validation. Default `10`. plot Logical indicating whether plot ROC curves. Default `TRUE`. palette String specifying color palette. Default `"jama"`. cols Optional vector colors ROC curves. `NULL`, uses default palette.

title: Build Prognostic Models Using LASSO and Ridge Regression — PrognosticModel
text: list containing: lasso_result Results LASSO model including coefficients AUC ridge_result Results Ridge model including coefficients AUC train.x Training data sample IDs

title: Regression Result Computation — RegressionResult
text: Computes regression results coefficients lambda.min lambda.1se, evaluates AUC binomial outcomes. Returns comprehensive summary model performance training testing datasets.

title: Regression Result Computation — RegressionResult
code: RegressionResult(train.x, train.y, test.x, test.y, model)

title: Regression Result Computation — RegressionResult
text: train.x Training predictors matrix. train.y Training outcomes (binary factor). test.x Testing predictors matrix. test.y Testing outcomes (binary factor). model Fitted cv.glmnet model object.

title: Regression Result Computation — RegressionResult
text: List containing: model fitted cv.glmnet model coefs Data frame feature names coefficients lambda.min lambda.1se AUC Matrix AUC values train/test sets lambda values

title: Regression Result Computation — RegressionResult
code: if (requireNamespace("glmnet", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(123)   train_data <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 10), ncol = 10)   train_outcome <- rbinom(100, 1, 0.5)   test_data <- matrix(rnorm(50 * 10), ncol = 10)   test_outcome <- rbinom(50, 1, 0.5)   fitted_model <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(train_data, train_outcome, family = "binomial", nfolds = 5)   results <- RegressionResult(     train.x = train_data, train.y = train_outcome,     test.x = test_data, test.y = test_outcome, model = fitted_model   ) }

what: Remove Batch Effect of Expression Set — RemoveBatchEffect
title: Remove Batch Effect of Expression Set — RemoveBatchEffect
text: Removes batch effects two gene expression datasets, typically representing different sample types cancer cells immune cells. Uses ComBat sva package batch correction.

title: Remove Batch Effect of Expression Set — RemoveBatchEffect
code: RemoveBatchEffect(cancer.exp, immune.exp, immune.cellType)

title: Remove Batch Effect of Expression Set — RemoveBatchEffect
text: cancer.exp Matrix data frame. Cancer cell expression data genes rows samples columns. immune.exp Matrix data frame. Immune cell expression data genes rows samples columns. immune.cellType Vector. Cell type column `immune.exp`.

title: Remove Batch Effect of Expression Set — RemoveBatchEffect
text: list containing: 1 Batch effect corrected cancer expression data 2 Batch effect corrected immune expression data 3 Aggregated immune expression data (median per cell type)

title: Remove Batch Effect of Expression Set — RemoveBatchEffect
text: Bo Li

title: Remove Batch Effect of Expression Set — RemoveBatchEffect
code: set.seed(123) gene_names <- paste0("Gene", 1:100) sample_names_cancer <- paste0("CancerSample", 1:10) cancer.exp <- matrix(runif(1000, 1, 1000),   nrow = 100, ncol = 10,   dimnames = list(gene_names, sample_names_cancer) )  sample_names_immune <- paste0("ImmuneSample", 1:5) immune.exp <- matrix(runif(500, 1, 1000),   nrow = 100, ncol = 5,   dimnames = list(gene_names, sample_names_immune) )  immune.cellType <- c("T-cell", "B-cell", "T-cell", "NK-cell", "B-cell") names(immune.cellType) <- sample_names_immune  result <- RemoveBatchEffect(cancer.exp, immune.exp, immune.cellType) if (!is.null(result)) str(result) #> List of 3 #>  $ : num [1:100, 1:10] 290 777 395 872 927 ... #>   ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:100] "Gene1" "Gene2" "Gene3" "Gene4" ... #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:10] "CancerSample1" "CancerSample2" "CancerSample3" "CancerSample4" ... #>  $ : num [1:100, 1:5] 329 554 213 811 783 ... #>   ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:100] "Gene1" "Gene2" "Gene3" "Gene4" ... #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:5] "ImmuneSample1" "ImmuneSample2" "ImmuneSample3" "ImmuneSample4" ... #>  $ : num [1:100, 1:5] 329 554 213 811 783 ... #>   ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:100] "Gene1" "Gene2" "Gene3" "Gene4" ... #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:5] "ImmuneSample1" "ImmuneSample2" "ImmuneSample3" "ImmuneSample4" ...

title: Add Custom Download Mirror — add_iobr_mirror
text: Adds custom mirror URL default mirrors current session. mirror URL base URL. contains 'github.com', treated GitHub proxy relative path IOBR releases appended. Otherwise, treated direct repository path.

title: Add Custom Download Mirror — add_iobr_mirror

what: Add Risk Score to Dataset — add_riskscore
title: Add Risk Score to Dataset — add_riskscore
text: Computes risk score observation based Cox proportional hazards regression binary logistic regression. function fits specified model returns dataset added risk score column.

title: Add Risk Score to Dataset — add_riskscore
code: add_riskscore(   input,   family = c("cox", "binary"),   target = NULL,   time = NULL,   status = NULL,   vars,   new_var_name = "riskscore" )

title: Add Risk Score to Dataset — add_riskscore
text: input Data frame containing variables analysis. family Character string specifying model family: `"cox"` Cox proportional hazards regression `"binary"` logistic regression. Default `"cox"`. target Character string specifying target variable name. Required `family = "binary"`. time Character string specifying time--event variable name. Required `family = "cox"`. status Character string specifying event status variable name. Required `family = "cox"`. vars Character vector variable names include model. new_var_name Character string specifying name new risk score column. Default `"riskscore"`.

title: Add Risk Score to Dataset — add_riskscore
text: Data frame identical `input` additional column containing   risk scores (linear predictors Cox models predicted probabilities   logistic models).

title: Add Risk Score to Dataset — add_riskscore
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Add Risk Score to Dataset — add_riskscore
code: set.seed(123) input_data <- data.frame(   time = rexp(100),   status = rbinom(100, 1, 0.5),   age = rnorm(100, 60, 10),   score1 = rnorm(100),   score2 = rnorm(100) ) result <- add_riskscore(   input_data,   time = "time", status = "status",   vars = c("age", "score1", "score2") ) head(result$riskscore) #> [1]  0.14482695 -0.01998538 -0.16480176  0.02682253 -0.15742721 -0.08938045

what: Annotate Gene Expression Matrix and Remove Duplicated Genes — anno_eset
title: Annotate Gene Expression Matrix and Remove Duplicated Genes — anno_eset
text: Annotates expression matrix gene symbols using provided annotation data, filters missing invalid symbols, handles duplicate gene entries, removes uninformative rows. function supports multiple aggregation methods resolving duplicate gene symbols.

title: Annotate Gene Expression Matrix and Remove Duplicated Genes — anno_eset
code: anno_eset(   eset,   annotation,   symbol = "symbol",   probe = "probe_id",   method = "mean" )

title: Annotate Gene Expression Matrix and Remove Duplicated Genes — anno_eset
text: eset Expression matrix ExpressionSet object containing gene expression data. annotation Data frame containing annotation information probes. Built-options include `anno_hug133plus2`, `anno_rnaseq`, `anno_illumina`. symbol Character string specifying column name `annotation` represents gene symbols. Default `"symbol"`. probe Character string specifying column name `annotation` represents probe identifiers. Default `"probe_id"`. method Character string specifying aggregation method duplicate gene symbols. Options `"mean"`, `"sum"`, `"sd"`. Default `"mean"`.

title: Annotate Gene Expression Matrix and Remove Duplicated Genes — anno_eset
text: Annotated cleaned gene expression matrix gene symbols   row names.

title: Annotate Gene Expression Matrix and Remove Duplicated Genes — anno_eset
text: function performs following operations: Filters probes missing symbols labeled `"NA_NA"` Matches probes expression set annotation data Merges annotation expression data Handles duplicate gene symbols using specified aggregation method Removes rows zeros, NAs, missing values first     column

title: Annotate Gene Expression Matrix and Remove Duplicated Genes — anno_eset
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Annotate Gene Expression Matrix and Remove Duplicated Genes — anno_eset
code: # Create a small example expression matrix eset_mat <- matrix(runif(100), nrow = 10, ncol = 10) rownames(eset_mat) <- paste0("Probe", 1:10) colnames(eset_mat) <- paste0("Sample", 1:10)  # Create a matching annotation data frame anno_df <- data.frame(   probe_id = paste0("Probe", 1:10),   symbol = c("Gene1", "Gene1", "Gene2", "Gene3", "Gene4",              "Gene5", "Gene6", "Gene7", "Gene8", "Gene9") )  # Annotate result <- anno_eset(eset = eset_mat, annotation = anno_df) #> ℹ Row number of original eset: 10 #> ✔ 100% of probes in expression set were annotated #> ℹ Found 1 duplicate symbol, using "mean" method #> ℹ Row number after filtering duplicated gene symbol: 9 head(result) #>         Sample1   Sample2    Sample3     Sample4   Sample5   Sample6   Sample7 #> Gene1 0.7619853 0.6753624 0.66960355 0.852364600 0.8665602 0.3637427 0.7706230 #> Gene2 0.8609894 0.7453418 0.81146366 0.668323644 0.3138426 0.6979466 0.9576697 #> Gene3 0.6735550 0.9425989 0.75916680 0.511314597 0.9594658 0.6844865 0.1586884 #> Gene8 0.1072947 0.5656543 0.92369921 0.003896343 0.8083863 0.8868626 0.9758897 #> Gene6 0.8917137 0.2594267 0.05088011 0.820474512 0.3839367 0.1372436 0.8697061 #> Gene5 0.6931989 0.2977231 0.38090370 0.903362288 0.5314093 0.5546818 0.8731514 #>          Sample8    Sample9  Sample10 #> Gene1 0.38917032 0.95657976 0.6232614 #> Gene2 0.09292582 0.37215774 0.9079464 #> Gene3 0.16180921 0.96261534 0.5694433 #> Gene8 0.56028054 0.50815808 0.7819689 #> Gene6 0.41525745 0.40994593 0.7620283 #> Gene5 0.34181444 0.06126261 0.1168276

what: Harmonize Two Data Frames by Column Structure — assimilate_data
title: Harmonize Two Data Frames by Column Structure — assimilate_data
text: Adds missing columns (filled `NA`) secondary data frame column set order match reference data frame. useful combining data frames different sources structure may missing columns.

title: Harmonize Two Data Frames by Column Structure — assimilate_data
code: assimilate_data(data_a, data_b)

title: Harmonize Two Data Frames by Column Structure — assimilate_data
text: data_a Data frame. Reference data frame whose column structure matched. data_b Data frame. Data frame conformed `data_a`.

title: Harmonize Two Data Frames by Column Structure — assimilate_data
text: Data frame `data_b` added missing columns (NA-filled)   reordered match `data_a`.

title: Harmonize Two Data Frames by Column Structure — assimilate_data
code: # Create reference data frame pdata_a <- data.frame(   A = 1:5, B = 2:6, C = 3:7, D = 4:8, E = 5:9 )  # Create data frame with subset of columns pdata_b <- data.frame(A = 1:3, C = 4:6, E = 7:9)  # Harmonize pdata_b to match pdata_a structure pdata_b_harmonized <- assimilate_data(data_a = pdata_a, data_b = pdata_b) #> ℹ Adding 2 missing columns: "B" and "D" print(names(pdata_b_harmonized)) # Now has A, B, C, D, E #> [1] "A" "B" "C" "D" "E"

title: Batch Correlation Analysis — batch_cor
text: Performs correlation analysis target variable multiple feature variables. Computes correlation coefficients, p-values, adjusts multiple testing using Benjamini-Hochberg method.

title: Batch Correlation Analysis — batch_cor
code: batch_cor(data, target, feature, method = c("spearman", "pearson", "kendall"))

title: Batch Correlation Analysis — batch_cor
text: data Data frame containing target feature variables. target Character string specifying name target variable. feature Character vector specifying names feature variables correlate target. method Character string specifying correlation method. Options `"spearman"`, `"pearson"`, `"kendall"`. Default `"spearman"`.

title: Batch Correlation Analysis — batch_cor
text: Tibble containing following columns feature: sig_names Feature name p.value Raw p-value statistic Correlation coefficient p.adj Adjusted p-value (Benjamini-Hochberg) log10pvalue Negative log10-transformed p-value stars Significance stars: **** p<0.0001, *** p<0.001,     ** p<0.01, * p<0.05, + p<0.5

title: Batch Correlation Analysis — batch_cor
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Batch Correlation Analysis — batch_cor
code: # Create a small example dataset set.seed(123) data_df <- as.data.frame(matrix(runif(100 * 10), 100, 10)) colnames(data_df) <- paste0("Signature", 1:10)  # Perform batch correlation results <- batch_cor(   data = data_df,   target = "Signature1",   feature = colnames(data_df)[2:10] ) #> ℹ Computing spearman correlation for 9 features #> ✔ Correlation analysis complete head(results) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   sig_names  p.value statistic p.adj log10pvalue stars #>   <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl> <fct> #> 1 Signature9  0.0514   -0.195  0.462       1.29  "+"   #> 2 Signature5  0.171    -0.138  0.698       0.766 "+"   #> 3 Signature2  0.380    -0.0887 0.698       0.420 "+"   #> 4 Signature6  0.406    -0.0841 0.698       0.392 "+"   #> 5 Signature4  0.438    -0.0784 0.698       0.358 "+"   #> 6 Signature3  0.532    -0.0633 0.698       0.274 ""

title: Batch Kruskal-Wallis Test — batch_kruskal
text: Performs Kruskal-Wallis rank sum tests multiple continuous features across different groups. Computes p-values, adjusts multiple testing, ranks features significance.

title: Batch Kruskal-Wallis Test — batch_kruskal
code: batch_kruskal(data, group, feature = NULL, feature_manipulation = FALSE)

title: Batch Kruskal-Wallis Test — batch_kruskal
text: data Data frame containing dataset analysis. group Character string specifying name grouping variable. feature Character vector specifying names feature variables test. `NULL`, user prompted select features (interactive mode ). Default `NULL`. feature_manipulation Logical indicating whether apply feature manipulation filter valid features. Default `FALSE`.

title: Batch Kruskal-Wallis Test — batch_kruskal
text: Tibble containing: sig_names Feature name p.value Raw p-value Kruskal-Wallis test statistic Test statistic (chi-squared) p.adj Adjusted p-value (Benjamini-Hochberg) log10pvalue Negative log10-transformed p-value stars Significance stars: **** p<0.0001, *** p<0.001,     ** p<0.01, * p<0.05, + p<0.5 group columns Mean-centered values group

title: Batch Kruskal-Wallis Test — batch_kruskal
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Batch Kruskal-Wallis Test — batch_kruskal
code: # Create small example data set.seed(123) test_data <- data.frame(   Gender = rep(c("Male", "Female"), each = 50),   Signature1 = rnorm(100),   Signature2 = rnorm(100) ) # Test features by gender res <- batch_kruskal(   data = test_data,   group = "Gender",   feature = c("Signature1", "Signature2") ) #> ℹ Groups: 2 ("Male" and "Female") #> ℹ Features: 2 #> ✔ Kruskal-Wallis test complete head(res) #> # A tibble: 2 × 8 #>   sig_names  p.value statistic Female    Male p.adj log10pvalue stars #>   <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl> <fct> #> 1 Signature2   0.128     2.32  0.146  -0.146  0.255       0.894 +     #> 2 Signature1   0.482     0.494 0.0560 -0.0560 0.482       0.317 +

what: Batch Calculation of Partial Correlation Coefficients — batch_pcc
title: Batch Calculation of Partial Correlation Coefficients — batch_pcc
text: Computes partial correlation coefficients multiple features target variable controlling interference (confounding) variable. Adjusts p-values multiple testing using Benjamini-Hochberg method.

title: Batch Calculation of Partial Correlation Coefficients — batch_pcc
code: batch_pcc(   input,   interferenceid,   target,   features,   method = c("pearson", "spearman", "kendall") )

title: Batch Calculation of Partial Correlation Coefficients — batch_pcc
text: input Data frame containing feature variables, target variable, interference variable. interferenceid Character string specifying column name interference (confounding) variable control . target Character string specifying column name target variable. features Character vector specifying column names feature variables correlate target. method Character string specifying correlation method. Options `"pearson"`, `"spearman"`, `"kendall"`. Default `"pearson"`.

title: Batch Calculation of Partial Correlation Coefficients — batch_pcc
text: Tibble containing following columns feature: sig_names Feature name p.value Raw p-value statistic Partial correlation coefficient p.adj Adjusted p-value (Benjamini-Hochberg method) log10pvalue Negative log10-transformed p-value stars Significance stars: **** p.adj<0.0001, *** p.adj<0.001,     ** p.adj<0.01, * p.adj<0.05, + p.adj<0.5

title: Batch Calculation of Partial Correlation Coefficients — batch_pcc
text: Rongfang Shen

title: Batch Calculation of Partial Correlation Coefficients — batch_pcc
code: # Create small example data set.seed(123) test_data <- data.frame(   TumorPurity = runif(100),   TargetVar = rnorm(100),   Signature1 = rnorm(100),   Signature2 = rnorm(100) ) # Calculate partial correlations controlling for tumor purity res <- batch_pcc(   input = test_data,   interferenceid = "TumorPurity",   target = "TargetVar",   method = "pearson",   features = c("Signature1", "Signature2") ) #> ℹ Computing pearson partial correlation for #> ✔ Partial correlation analysis complete head(res) #> # A tibble: 2 × 6 #>   sig_names  p.value statistic p.adj log10pvalue stars #>   <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl> <fct> #> 1 Signature1   0.387   -0.0878 0.775      0.412  ""    #> 2 Signature2   0.877   -0.0158 0.877      0.0570 ""

title: Batch Signature Survival Plot — batch_sig_surv_plot
text: Generates Kaplan-Meier survival plots multiple projects cohorts based signature scores. Automatically determines optimal cutpoints signature stratification creates publication-ready survival curves.

title: Batch Signature Survival Plot — batch_sig_surv_plot
code: batch_sig_surv_plot(   input_pdata,   signature,   id = "ID",   column_of_project = "ProjectID",   project = NULL,   time = "time",   status = "status",   time_type = "day",   break_month = "auto",   palette = "jama",   cols = NULL,   mini_sig = "score",   save_path = NULL,   show_col = TRUE,   fig_type = "pdf" )

title: Batch Signature Survival Plot — batch_sig_surv_plot
text: input_pdata Data frame containing survival data signature scores. signature Character string specifying column name target signature survival analysis. id Character string specifying column name containing unique identifiers. Default `"ID"`. column_of_project Character string specifying column name containing project identifiers. Default `"ProjectID"`. project Character string vector specifying project name(s) analyze. `NULL`, projects analyzed. Default `NULL`. time Character string specifying column name containing time--event data. Default `"time"`. status Character string specifying column name containing event status. Default `"status"`. time_type Character string specifying time unit. Options `"day"` `"month"`. Default `"day"`. break_month Numeric value `"auto"` specifying interval time axis breaks months. Default `"auto"`. palette Character string specifying color palette. Default `"jama"`. cols Character vector custom colors. `NULL`, palette used. Default `NULL`. mini_sig Character string signature label legend. Default `"score"`. save_path Character string `NULL`. Directory path saving plots. `NULL`, plots saved. Default `NULL`. show_col Logical indicating whether display color information. Default `TRUE`. fig_type Character string specifying output file format (`"pdf"`, `"png"`, etc.). Default `"pdf"`.

title: Batch Signature Survival Plot — batch_sig_surv_plot
text: Data frame containing combined survival analysis results   projects.

title: Batch Signature Survival Plot — batch_sig_surv_plot
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Batch Signature Survival Plot — batch_sig_surv_plot
code: set.seed(123) test_pdata <- data.frame(   ID = paste0("S", 1:20),   ProjectID = rep("P1", 20),   OS_time = runif(20, 1, 60),   OS_status = sample(c(0, 1), 20, replace = TRUE),   Marker = rnorm(20) ) result <- batch_sig_surv_plot(   input_pdata = test_pdata, signature = "Marker",   id = "ID", column_of_project = "ProjectID",   time = "OS_time", status = "OS_status", time_type = "month" ) #> ℹ Processing project: "P1" #> ℹ Survival follow-up time range: 3.48 to 57.45 months #> ℹ Best cutoff for "Marker": 0.69 #> ✔ Best cutoff for "Marker": 0.689 #> ℹ High Marker: 5 #> ℹ Low Marker: 15 #> ℹ Maximum follow-up time is 57.5 months; divided into 6 sections #> Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0. #> ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead. #> ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the ggpubr package. #>   Please report the issue at <https://github.com/kassambara/ggpubr/issues>. #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • colour : "Strata" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • colour : "Strata" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • colour : "Strata" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • colour : "Strata" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • colour : "Strata" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • colour : "Strata" if (!is.null(result)) head(result) #>   ID      time status     Marker group3 group2 bestcutoff #> 1 S1 17.967074      0 -1.0678237    Low    Low        Low #> 2 S2 47.510003      1 -0.2179749 Middle    Low        Low #> 3 S3 25.129638      0 -1.0260044    Low    Low        Low #> 4 S4 53.098027      0 -0.7288912    Low    Low        Low #> 5 S5 56.487570      0 -0.6250393    Low    Low        Low #> 6 S6  3.687833      0 -1.6866933    Low    Low        Low

title: Batch Survival Analysis — batch_surv
text: Performs Cox proportional hazards regression analysis multiple variables. Optionally determines optimal cutoffs dichotomize continuous predictors modeling. Returns hazard ratios, confidence intervals, p-values variable.

title: Batch Survival Analysis — batch_surv
code: batch_surv(   pdata,   variable,   time = "time",   status = "status",   best_cutoff = FALSE )

title: Batch Survival Analysis — batch_surv
text: pdata Data frame containing survival time, event status, predictor variables. variable Character vector specifying names predictor variables analyze. time Character string specifying column name containing follow-time. Default `"time"`. status Character string specifying column name containing event status (1 = event occurred, 0 = censored). Default `"status"`. best_cutoff Logical indicating whether compute optimal cutoffs continuous variables analyze dichotomized versions. Default `FALSE`.

title: Batch Survival Analysis — batch_surv
text: Data frame containing hazard ratios (HR), 95   p-values variable, sorted p-value.

title: Batch Survival Analysis — batch_surv
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Batch Survival Analysis — batch_surv
code: # Create small example data set.seed(123) test_data <- data.frame(   OS_time = runif(100, 0, 100),   OS_status = sample(c(0, 1), 100, replace = TRUE),   Signature1 = rnorm(100),   Signature2 = rnorm(100) ) batch_surv(   pdata = test_data,   variable = c("Signature1", "Signature2"),   time = "OS_time",   status = "OS_status" ) #> # A tibble: 2 × 5 #>   ID             P    HR CI_low_0.95 CI_up_0.95 #>   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Signature2 0.317 0.862       0.644       1.15 #> 2 Signature1 0.365 0.863       0.628       1.19

title: Calculate Signature Score — calculate_sig_score
text: Main interface calculating signature scores gene expression data. Supports PCA, z-score, ssGSEA, integration methods.

title: Calculate Signature Score — calculate_sig_score
code: calculate_sig_score(   pdata = NULL,   eset,   signature = NULL,   method = c("pca", "ssgsea", "zscore", "integration"),   mini_gene_count = 3,   column_of_sample = "ID",   print_gene_proportion = FALSE,   print_filtered_signatures = FALSE,   adjust_eset = FALSE,   parallel.size = 1L,   ... )

title: Calculate Signature Score — calculate_sig_score
text: pdata Phenotype data. `NULL`, created `eset` column names. eset Expression matrix (CPM, TPM, RPKM, FPKM, etc.). signature List gene signatures. Can also character string naming built-signature collection (e.g., `"signature_collection"`, `"signature_tme"`, `"go_bp"`, `"kegg"`, `"hallmark"`). method Scoring method: `"pca"`, `"ssgsea"`, `"zscore"`, `"integration"`. Default `"pca"`. mini_gene_count Minimum genes required per signature. Default 3 (5 ssGSEA). column_of_sample Column sample IDs `pdata`. Default `"ID"`. print_gene_proportion Logical: print gene coverage. Default `FALSE`. print_filtered_signatures Logical: print filtered signatures. Default `FALSE`. adjust_eset Logical: clean problematic features. Default `FALSE`. parallel.size Parallel workers ssGSEA. Default 1. ... Additional arguments passed specific methods.

title: Calculate Signature Score — calculate_sig_score
text: Tibble phenotype data signature scores.

title: Calculate Signature Score — calculate_sig_score
text: Hänzelmann S, Castelo R, Guinney J. GSVA: gene set variation     analysis. BMC Bioinformatics. 2013;14:7. Mariathasan S, et al. TGF\(\beta\) attenuates tumour response PD-L1 blockade.     Nature. 2018;554:544-548.

title: Calculate Signature Score — calculate_sig_score
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Calculate Signature Score — calculate_sig_score
code: set.seed(123) eset <- matrix(rnorm(1000), nrow = 100, ncol = 10) rownames(eset) <- paste0("Gene", 1:100) colnames(eset) <- paste0("Sample", 1:10) signature <- list(   Signature1 = paste0("Gene", 1:10),   Signature2 = paste0("Gene", 11:20) ) # \donttest{ result <- calculate_sig_score(eset = eset, signature = signature, method = "pca") #> ℹ Calculating signature scores using PCA method #> ℹ Log2 transformation not necessary (data appears to already be log-scaled) #> ℹ Calculating scores for 2 signature(s) # }

title: Calculate Signature Score Using Integration Method — calculate_sig_score_integration
text: pdata Data frame phenotype data. `NULL`, created `eset` column names. eset Expression matrix (genes rows, samples columns). signature List gene signatures. mini_gene_count Minimum genes required per signature. Default 3. column_of_sample Column `pdata` sample IDs. Default `"ID"`. adjust_eset Logical: remove problematic features. Default `FALSE`. parallel.size Number parallel workers. Default 1.

what: Visualize Cell Fractions as Stacked Bar Chart — cell_bar_plot
title: Visualize Cell Fractions as Stacked Bar Chart — cell_bar_plot
text: Creates stacked bar charts visualize tumor microenvironment (TME) cell fractions. Supports batch visualization deconvolution results methods CIBERSORT, EPIC, quanTIseq.

title: Visualize Cell Fractions as Stacked Bar Chart — cell_bar_plot
code: cell_bar_plot(   input,   id = "ID",   title = "Cell Fraction",   features = NULL,   pattern = NULL,   legend.position = "bottom",   coord_flip = TRUE,   palette = 3,   show_col = FALSE,   cols = NULL )

title: Visualize Cell Fractions as Stacked Bar Chart — cell_bar_plot
text: input Data frame containing deconvolution results. id Character string specifying column name containing sample identifiers. Default "ID". title Character string specifying plot title. Default "Cell Fraction". features Character vector specifying column names representing cell types plot. NULL, columns selected based `pattern`. Default NULL. pattern Character string regular expression match column names automatic feature selection. Used `features` NULL. Default NULL. legend.position Character string specifying legend position ("bottom", "top", "left", "right"). Default "bottom". coord_flip Logical indicating whether flip plot coordinates using `coord_flip()`. Default TRUE. palette Integer specifying color palette use. Default 3. show_col Logical indicating whether display color information. Default FALSE. cols Character vector custom colors. NULL, palette used. Default NULL.

title: Visualize Cell Fractions as Stacked Bar Chart — cell_bar_plot
text: ggplot2 object representing stacked bar chart.

title: Visualize Cell Fractions as Stacked Bar Chart — cell_bar_plot

title: Visualize Cell Fractions as Stacked Bar Chart — cell_bar_plot
code: set.seed(123) input_data <- data.frame(   ID = paste0("Sample", 1:10),   Cell_A = runif(10, 0, 0.4),   Cell_B = runif(10, 0, 0.3),   Cell_C = runif(10, 0, 0.3) ) cell_bar_plot(input = input_data, id = "ID", features = c("Cell_A", "Cell_B", "Cell_C")) #> ℹ Available categories: box, continue2, continue, random, heatmap, heatmap3, tidyheatmap #> ℹ Random palettes: 1 (palette1), 2 (palette2), 3 (palette3), 4 (palette4)

what: Combine Phenotype Data and Expression Set — combine_pd_eset
title: Combine Phenotype Data and Expression Set — combine_pd_eset
text: Merges phenotype data expression matrix matching sample IDs. Optionally filters features, applies feature manipulation, scales expression data combining.

title: Combine Phenotype Data and Expression Set — combine_pd_eset
code: combine_pd_eset(   eset,   pdata,   id_pdata = "ID",   feas = NULL,   feature_manipulation = TRUE,   scale = TRUE,   choose_who_when_duplicate = c("eset", "pdata") )

title: Combine Phenotype Data and Expression Set — combine_pd_eset
text: eset Expression matrix genes/features rows samples columns. pdata Data frame containing phenotype/clinical data. id_pdata Character string specifying column name `pdata` containing sample identifiers. Default `"ID"`. feas Character vector specifying features include `eset`. `NULL`, features used. Default `NULL`. feature_manipulation Logical indicating whether apply feature manipulation filter valid features. Default `TRUE`. scale Logical indicating whether scale (standardize) expression data. Default `TRUE`. choose_who_when_duplicate Character string specifying data prefer duplicate columns exist. Options `"eset"` `"pdata"`. Default `"eset"`.

title: Combine Phenotype Data and Expression Set — combine_pd_eset
text: Data frame combining phenotype data (transposed) expression data,   samples rows features/phenotypes columns.

title: Combine Phenotype Data and Expression Set — combine_pd_eset
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Combine Phenotype Data and Expression Set — combine_pd_eset
code: set.seed(123) eset <- matrix(rnorm(1000), nrow = 100, ncol = 10) rownames(eset) <- paste0("Gene", 1:100) colnames(eset) <- paste0("Sample", 1:10) pdata <- data.frame(   ID = colnames(eset),   group = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 5),   age = rnorm(10, 50, 10) ) result <- combine_pd_eset(eset = eset, pdata = pdata, scale = FALSE) #> ℹ Retained 100 features after QC #> ✔ Combined data: 10 samples x 103 variables dim(result) #> [1]  10 103

what: Convert Read Counts to Transcripts Per Million (TPM) — count2tpm
title: Convert Read Counts to Transcripts Per Million (TPM) — count2tpm
text: Transforms gene expression count data Transcripts Per Million (TPM) values, normalizing gene length library size. Supports multiple gene ID types can retrieve gene length information BioMart use local datasets.

title: Convert Read Counts to Transcripts Per Million (TPM) — count2tpm
code: count2tpm(   countMat,   idType = "Ensembl",   org = c("hsa", "mmus"),   source = c("local", "biomart"),   effLength = NULL,   id = "id",   gene_symbol = "symbol",   length = "eff_length",   check_data = FALSE )

title: Convert Read Counts to Transcripts Per Million (TPM) — count2tpm
text: countMat Numeric matrix raw read counts genes rows samples columns. idType Character string specifying gene identifier type. Options `"Ensembl"`, `"Entrez"`, `"Symbol"`. Default `"Ensembl"`. org Character string specifying organism. Options include `"hsa"` (human) `"mmus"` (mouse). Default `"hsa"`. source Character string specifying source gene length information. Options `"biomart"` (retrieve Ensembl BioMart) `"local"` (use local dataset). Default `"local"`. effLength Data frame containing effective gene length information. `NULL`, lengths retrieved based `source`. Default `NULL`. id Character string specifying column name `effLength` containing gene identifiers. Default `"id"`. gene_symbol Character string specifying column name `effLength` containing gene symbols. Default `"symbol"`. length Character string specifying column name `effLength` containing gene lengths. Default `"eff_length"`. check_data Logical indicating whether check missing values count matrix. Default `FALSE`.

title: Convert Read Counts to Transcripts Per Million (TPM) — count2tpm
text: Data frame TPM-normalized expression values genes rows   samples columns. Gene identifiers converted gene symbols   output, regardless input `idType`.

title: Convert Read Counts to Transcripts Per Million (TPM) — count2tpm
text: Wubing Zhang, Dongqiang Zeng, Yiran Fang

title: Convert Read Counts to Transcripts Per Million (TPM) — count2tpm
code: # Simulated count data set.seed(123) count_matrix <- matrix(   rpois(100, lambda = 10),   ncol = 5,   dimnames = list(     paste0("ENSG00000", 101:120),     paste0("Sample", 1:5)   ) ) # Simulated effective length data eff_len <- data.frame(   id = paste0("ENSG00000", 101:120),   symbol = paste0("Gene", 1:20),   eff_length = runif(20, 500, 5000) ) # Transform to TPM using local gene annotation eset <- count2tpm(   countMat = count_matrix, effLength = eff_len,   id = "id", length = "eff_length", gene_symbol = "symbol" ) #> ℹ No duplicate gene symbols found. head(eset) #>         Sample1   Sample2   Sample3   Sample4   Sample5 #> Gene1  17872.27  15587.34  18209.61  24387.20  27170.38 #> Gene2  25679.75  26544.19  23257.31  33978.89  34701.99 #> Gene3  42051.04  13971.40  36724.09  26826.92  30910.36 #> Gene4 146098.96 101936.44 133970.97 130487.57 136681.51 #> Gene5  47308.28  33008.03  48201.25  32863.57  35407.06 #> Gene6  61007.04  37836.40  37295.14  28253.11  53269.60

title: Deconvolve Using CIBERSORT — deconvo_cibersort
text: eset Expression matrix gene symbols row names. project Optional project name. Default `NULL`. arrays Logical: optimized microarray data. Default `FALSE`. perm Permutations statistical analysis. Default 1000. absolute Logical: run absolute mode. Default `FALSE`. abs_method Method absolute mode: `"sig.score"` `".sumto1"`. Default `"sig.score"`. parallel Enable parallel execution. Default `FALSE`. num_cores Number cores parallel mode. Default 2. seed Random seed reproducibility. Default `NULL`.

title: Deconvolve Using CIBERSORT — deconvo_cibersort

title: Deconvolve Using CIBERSORT — deconvo_cibersort
text: Dongqiang Zeng

title: Deconvolve Using CIBERSORT — deconvo_cibersort
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lm22 <- load_data("lm22") if (!is.null(lm22)) {   set.seed(123)   sim_eset <- matrix(rnorm(nrow(lm22) * 2), nrow(lm22), 2)   rownames(sim_eset) <- rownames(lm22)   colnames(sim_eset) <- paste0("Sample", 1:2)   result <- deconvo_cibersort(eset = sim_eset, project = "TCGA-STAD", perm = 10)   if (!is.null(result)) head(result) } } # }

title: Main TME Deconvolution Function — deconvo_tme
text: eset Gene expression matrix HGNC symbols row names. project Optional project name. Default `NULL`. method Deconvolution method. See [tme_deconvolution_methods]. arrays Logical: microarray-optimized mode. Default `FALSE`. tumor Logical: tumor-optimized mode (EPIC). Default `TRUE`. perm Permutations (CIBERSORT/SVR). Default 1000. reference Custom reference matrix (SVR/lsei). scale_reference Logical: scale reference (SVR/lsei). plot Logical: generate plots (IPS). Default `FALSE`. scale_mrna Logical: mRNA correction (quanTIseq/EPIC). group_list Cancer types TIMER (vector). platform Platform ESTIMATE. Default `"affymetrix"`. absolute.mode Logical: absolute mode (CIBERSORT/SVR). Default `FALSE`. abs.method Absolute mode method. Default `"sig.score"`. ... Additional arguments passed method.

what: Use quanTIseq to Deconvolute a Gene Expression Matrix — deconvolute_quantiseq.default
title: Use quanTIseq to Deconvolute a Gene Expression Matrix — deconvolute_quantiseq.default
text: Deconvolutes gene expression data estimate immune cell fractions using quanTIseq method. Source code https://github.com/FFinotello/quanTIseq.

title: Use quanTIseq to Deconvolute a Gene Expression Matrix — deconvolute_quantiseq.default
code: deconvolute_quantiseq.default(   mix.mat,   arrays = FALSE,   signame = "TIL10",   tumor = FALSE,   mRNAscale = TRUE,   method = c("lsei", "hampel", "huber", "bisquare"),   rmgenes = "unassigned" )

title: Use quanTIseq to Deconvolute a Gene Expression Matrix — deconvolute_quantiseq.default
text: mix.mat Data frame matrix. Gene expression matrix gene symbols first column sample IDs first row. Expression data must non-log scale (TPM RNA-seq expression values microarrays). arrays Logical. Whether expression data microarrays. Default FALSE. TRUE, rmgenes parameter set "none". signame Character. Name signature matrix. Currently "TIL10" available. Default "TIL10". tumor Logical. Whether expression data tumor samples. TRUE, signature genes high expression tumor samples removed. Default FALSE. mRNAscale Logical. Whether cell fractions must scaled account cell-type-specific mRNA content. Default TRUE. method Character. Deconvolution method: "hampel", "huber", "bisquare" robust regression, "lsei" constrained least squares. Default "lsei". rmgenes Character. Genes remove: "unassigned" (default), "default", "none", "path".

title: Use quanTIseq to Deconvolute a Gene Expression Matrix — deconvolute_quantiseq.default
text: Data frame cell fractions sample.

title: Use quanTIseq to Deconvolute a Gene Expression Matrix — deconvolute_quantiseq.default
text: F. Finotello, C. Mayer, C. Plattner, G. Laschober, D. Rieder, H. Hackl, . Krogsdam, W. Posch, D. Wilflingseder, S. Sopper, M. Jsselsteijn, D. Johnsons, Y. Xu, Y. Wang, M. E. Sanders, M. V. Estrada, P. Ericsson-Gonzalez, J. Balko, N. F. de Miranda, Z. Trajanoski. "quanTIseq: quantifying immune contexture human tumors". bioRxiv 223180. https://doi.org/10.1101/223180.

title: Use quanTIseq to Deconvolute a Gene Expression Matrix — deconvolute_quantiseq.default

title: Use quanTIseq to Deconvolute a Gene Expression Matrix — deconvolute_quantiseq.default
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ quantiseq_data <- load_data("quantiseq_data") if (!is.null(quantiseq_data)) {   common_genes <- rownames(quantiseq_data$TIL10_signature)   tpm_matrix <- as.data.frame(matrix(     abs(rnorm(length(common_genes) * 2, mean = 5, sd = 2)),     nrow = length(common_genes), ncol = 2   ))   rownames(tpm_matrix) <- common_genes   colnames(tpm_matrix) <- paste0("Sample", 1:2)   results <- deconvolute_quantiseq.default(mix.mat = tpm_matrix)   if (!is.null(results)) head(results) } } # }

what: Deconvolute Tumor Microenvironment Using TIMER — deconvolute_timer.default
title: Deconvolute Tumor Microenvironment Using TIMER — deconvolute_timer.default
text: Performs deconvolution tumor microenvironment using TIMER algorithm. Processes multiple cancer datasets, removes batch effects, estimates immune cell type abundances.

title: Deconvolute Tumor Microenvironment Using TIMER — deconvolute_timer.default
code: deconvolute_timer.default(args)

title: Deconvolute Tumor Microenvironment Using TIMER — deconvolute_timer.default
text: args List environment containing parameters: outdir Character. Output directory path. batch Character. File containing paths expression data     cancer types.

title: Deconvolute Tumor Microenvironment Using TIMER — deconvolute_timer.default
text: Matrix abundance scores different immune cell types across   multiple cancer samples.

title: Deconvolute Tumor Microenvironment Using TIMER — deconvolute_timer.default
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(123) immune <- load_data("immuneCuratedData") cancer_genes <- load_data("cancer_type_genes") if (!is.null(immune) && !is.null(cancer_genes)) {   gene_names <- unique(c(head(rownames(immune$genes), 30), cancer_genes[["stad"]]))   sample_names <- paste0("Sample", 1:2)   n_genes <- length(gene_names)   expr <- matrix(runif(n_genes * 2, 1, 100), n_genes, 2,                  dimnames = list(gene_names, sample_names))   tf <- tempfile(fileext = ".tsv")   write.table(as.data.frame(expr), tf, sep = "\t", quote = FALSE)   batch_tf <- tempfile(fileext = ".csv")   write.table(data.frame(path = tf, type = "stad"), batch_tf,               sep = ",", col.names = FALSE, row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE)   args <- list(outdir = tempdir(), batch = batch_tf)   result <- deconvolute_timer.default(args)   if (!is.null(result)) head(result) } } # }

Default 2. axis_text_size Size axis tick labels. Default 12. axis_angle Angle x-axis tick labels. Default 60. hjust Horizontal justification x-axis text. Default 1. legend.position Legend position: "none", "left", "right", "bottom", "top". Default "bottom". legend.direction Direction legend items: "horizontal" "vertical". Default 0.5. legend.size.text Size legend text labels. Default 10. legend.box Orientation legend box: "horizontal" "vertical". Default "horizontal".

title: Permutation Test for CIBERSORT — doPerm
text: Performs permutation-based sampling generate empirical null distribution correlation coefficients p-value calculation CIBERSORT analysis. Randomly samples mixture data create null distributions.

title: Permutation Test for CIBERSORT — doPerm
text: perm Integer. Number permutations perform (\(\ge\)100 recommended reliable p-value estimation). X Matrix data frame containing signature matrix (predictor variables). Y Numeric vector containing mixture sample expression. absolute Logical indicating whether use absolute space weights. abs_method String specifying method absolute space weights: `"sig.score"` `".sumto1"`. seed Integer. Random seed reproducibility. NULL (default), uses current random state.

title: Permutation Test for CIBERSORT — doPerm
text: List containing: dist Numeric vector correlation coefficients permutations

title: Permutation Test for CIBERSORT — doPerm
code: # Simulate data set.seed(123) X <- matrix(rnorm(100), nrow = 10) rownames(X) <- paste0("Gene", 1:10) colnames(X) <- paste0("Cell", 1:10) Y <- rnorm(10) names(Y) <- paste0("Gene", 1:10)  # Run permutation test result <- doPerm(10, X, Y, absolute = FALSE, abs_method = "sig.score") if (!is.null(result)) str(result) #> List of 1 #>  $ dist: num [1:10] 0.883 0.794 0.708 0.739 0.41 ...

title: estimateScore — estimateScore
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(123) si_geneset_data <- load_data("SI_geneset") if (!is.null(si_geneset_data)) {   gene_names <- unique(c(si_geneset_data[1, -1], si_geneset_data[2, -1]))   gene_names <- gene_names[!is.na(gene_names) & gene_names != ""]   gene_names <- head(gene_names, 200)   n_genes <- length(gene_names)   eset_sim <- as.data.frame(matrix(rnorm(n_genes * 2, mean = 5, sd = 1), n_genes, 2))   rownames(eset_sim) <- gene_names   colnames(eset_sim) <- c("Sample1", "Sample2")   eset_sim <- tibble::rownames_to_column(eset_sim, var = "symbol")   input_file <- tempfile(pattern = "estimate_", fileext = ".gct")   output_file <- tempfile(pattern = "estimate_score_", fileext = ".gct")   writeLines(c("#1.2", paste(nrow(eset_sim), ncol(eset_sim) - 1, sep = "\t")), input_file)   utils::write.table(eset_sim, input_file, sep = "\t", row.names = FALSE,                     col.names = TRUE, append = TRUE, quote = FALSE)   score_res <- estimateScore(input.ds = input_file, output.ds = output_file,                              platform = "affymetrix")   if (!isFALSE(score_res) && file.exists(output_file)) {     head(read.table(output_file, skip = 2, header = TRUE, sep = "\t"))   } } } # }
